speaker: interviewer
text: Today is December 12, 1996. The survivor is Dr. Leon Wells. His last name at birth was Weliczker. The interviewer is Ruth Meyer. The interview is taking place in Closter, New Jersey, in the United States. The interview will be conducted in English. I am Ruth Meyer, M-E-Y-E-R. Today is December 12, 1996. We are in Closter, New Jersey, in the United States, with Dr. Leon Weliczker Wells. The interview will be conducted in English.

speaker: interviewee
text: Dr. Wells, I'd like to begin by asking you to state your name and spell your last name for us, please.

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Leon Weliczker Wells. Weliczker-- W-E-L-I-C-Z-K-E-R. That was my original name. I kept it when I got my citizen papers as a middle name.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your last name, if you would spell it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Wells, W-E-L-L-S.

speaker: interviewer
text: And would you tell me, please, your date of birth?

speaker: interviewer
text: And your age?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm 71 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the city and country of your birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in Stojanow, near Lwow, in 1925, in Poland. It was Poland at this time, when I was born.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'd like to begin by having you tell me something about your family, what you remember of your grandparents, and your parents-- a little bit of their background and their story.

speaker: interviewee
text: I had a very big family. We lived in the small town-- what they call here in America a shtetl, but at this time, it was a small town-- near Lwow, and where my father's family was, not my mother's. My mother is originally from Sokol, not far from Stojanow. And my father was originally from-- no, my mother was from Kristianopel. And My father was from Sokol, also near Stojanow. And they moved to Stojanow after World War I. And it was about 1,000 Jewish families in this small town, where all Jews lived in center of the city. And the Ukrainian and Polish group lived outside the town, nearby. And it was divided practically into three parts-- equal amount of Jews, equal amount of Ukrainians, equal amount of Poles.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the Jewish population was about 1/3 of the city?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes, of the small town. And we had, as far as I know, since I was-- well, we had no problem between Jews and non-Jews, or any problems that we were afraid to go out in the night, or something. And that was about-- and most of the [? city ?] Jews were of the Hasidic background. Most of them belonged to the Belzer Rebbe, which was also not far from here. It was even-- he was even related to us, the Belzer Rebbe. And for the holidays, the men very often went to the Belzer Rebbe, left their family, and they-- and that was the normal life. And most of them were from artisans or small business people. There were no big businesses in Stojanow.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me your parents' names. And what did your father do for a living?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's name was Abraham Weliczker. And my mother's name was Hannah Weliczker. And her maiden name was Reiss, R-E-I-S-S. And my father was in the wood business-- lumber business-- and also an exporter of eggs. But lumber was mostly-- a lot of them was export to United States for the railroad.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he exported wood to the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes. And we-- in Poland, it was a law that you could not cut woods more than three acres at one time. Reseed it, and until they grow, and you had permission to cut another three acres. So we cut the three acres, and cut it into-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] into different kind of woods as it's needed, and so on. And my father was in this business. My grandfather even worked for my father. And that was--

speaker: interviewer
text: You had brothers and sisters.

speaker: interviewee
text: I had two brothers and four sisters, cousins, uncles, and all of the-- all of  them--

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me your brothers' names.

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother's name was Aaron. Aaron-- A-A-R-O-N. And my other brother's name was Jacob, Yacob.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: And my sisters was the oldest-- I was the second one in my family. My older sister was Ella, Elke. My second one was Rachel. And afterwards was Judith. And afterwards was [? Biene. ?] [? Biene ?] was the youngest, born in 1935, already in Lwow, after we moved in 1933 to Lwow.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, I think we'll go into what happened to them later on, as we get more into the story.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: But I'd like to hear a little bit about your home life and what your family relationship was among the family.

speaker: interviewee
text: Family relationship was a normal family relationship. My father was one of five children in Stojanow. All his brothers and sisters lived nearby. My grandfather-- my grandmother of my father's side lived nearby. My mother's side-- they stayed in Kristianopel, which was about 40, 50 kilometers from Stojanow. But at this time, it was a distance. We did not see them very often. But my father's family-- we were in the same town and-- and saw them very often. And that was-- and also my brothers, sisters-- it was a normal relationship between young brothers and sisters. I went to school, they went to school, and so on. But mostly, like at this time, girls were kept separate, and boys are separate. I did not mean by separate, but it was no really interaction too much. Because since I was three, I was taken to a cheder, a Jewish school, and learned every day. And so it was different responsibilities. And we grew up of different philosophies and responsibilities for boys and girls.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me a little bit about Shabbos in your house. What do you remember about preparations and observance of Shabbat?

speaker: interviewee
text: Shabbat was the absolute holy day which-- that you did not do anything on this day. The boys-- women never went to the synagogue, like here or something, or [NON-ENGLISH]. And so only the men went to the synagogue. So the father, with his boys, went to the synagogue. In this way, I believe, kept us also separation between-- brothers and sisters-- at home, because we had different directions to go on, to do. And we were going to the synagogue every Friday night and Saturday. And that was our-- and afterwards, we had our lunch. And afterwards, we had our rest of the day. And the father, mother, and the children played outside. And we all knew about the holy day of [? Shabbat. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about your relationships with other children who were not Jewish. You said that the relationship was fairly stable?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a fairly stable, but a very unknown separation of-- of Jews and non-Jews. It was separate living, separate background, separate responsibility for the future, separate ideas. For example, I-- I was going to cheder. They? I don't know. I never knew non-Jewish children before the war, when I was small. I never played--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go to a public school in addition to cheder?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to a public school, but when I was seven. But until the age of seven, I never saw or never met a non-Jewish boy or-- Jews, in reality, created-- there was like a ghetto life, which was created by ourselves. We had a separate-- a separate life. We had our own-- to go to Friday to the-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] to live every day in a separate life. We could not eat, or could not see, or could not associate with non-Jews. Because we knew, as children, that to look-- if a Gentile looked at wine, opened wine, it was non-Kosher. So we couldn't even get together in our home to invite. Because they couldn't even walk in door. We had to have a synagogue. We had to-- and we were taught that you are separated. And separation we were taught from the childhood.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about the observance of other holidays? Do you remember preparations for Pesach?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. For Pesach-- for Pesach, it was started a month before already. It's a cleaning of the house, and-- and washing. And there was everything thrown out of the house-- taken out of the house. Even the dishes-- separate dishes, new dishes, and so on. So it was a complete-- our whole life turned around, around the holidays-- of Pesach. After Pesach came [? Shavuos. ?] After [? Shavuos, ?] came Rosh Hashanah, Yom Kippur, and afterwards Sukkot. It was continuously something to prepare, something to look to it, and so on, for the children and the parents, because it was a very important happening of the year in the life.

speaker: interviewer
text: On Pesach, did you have a Seder to which other family members came?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, nobody came to our Pesach Seder. Because everybody had a Seder for his own. Everybody had to have, with his family, a Seder. And-- and the only time when we had a Seder with other family members was in my case never, because my father never left the family for Pesach or for high holidays to the Rebbe, to the Belzer Rebbe. But there were families where the men left to be with the Rebbe. And the families stayed at home behind. So they were invited with other parts of the family. But normally, not too much. Because like my uncle that went to the Rebbe, and his wife and daughter spent the Pesach with his-- with her father and mother. So it wasn't to go to the brother, or to the family. And everybody had a big family, like we had, [INAUDIBLE] example, we were seven children, and two grown-ups, and a helper-- a cleaning woman. That was 10 people in the house to start. So it was, by itself, a Passover Seder, a Passover-- or any holiday, like [INAUDIBLE], a big table. It wasn't that you invited people. The only people that you invited, if it happened-- it normal did not happen on holidays. It happened on Shabbat-- was a poor man to come to dinner for Friday night. After the synagogue, you took him to dinner to your house. But otherwise, it was never a case that you had people or friends over for the holidays. Because they had their own holidays.

speaker: interviewer
text: On Pesach, where did the matzo come from?

speaker: interviewee
text: I came in at a time-- when I was a child, what I had, it was after the fight between two groups if machine matzos are Kosher or non-Kosher. And we started already with machine matzos. But we baked also matzos in every town. It was you baked. You were a part of baking of the matzos. And even the children went to bake matzos. And these-- and everybody got the matzos from the bakery-- you, your own. You and other people of town baked the matzos.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'd like you to explain to me, if you can, in a little more detail, the impact and the influence of the Rebbe-- in your case, the Belzer Rebbe.

speaker: interviewee
text: The influence of the Rebbe was very great-- not only great, but I feel without the Rebbe, my family would have survived. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] It was-- the Rebbe had the absolute influence of life and death of the-- of not real the death, but the life, of every family. My father that already questioned the idea of the Rebbe-- and he was one of this family-- but basically, couldn't go against it. He did not allow any of his followers to leave for America or Israel, or any-- because the [? Galut ?] was a part of God's will. Everything that happened was God's will. And we had to stay in Poland at any time, even if the Germans will come in. Because God is not going to forsake his people. And as much as we til today don't believe or question the idea of having possibilities to come to America, or to any other country, there was a big possibility. Because if you had a certain amount of money, you did not need even invitation to America. You could go in, as a capitalist. And my father had some money. But the Rebbe said, God forbid. You don't leave the country. And so the influence is very-- and while he did not believe in it 100%, but he said, if the Rebbe said, in absolute terms, God forbid, you were not to break it. And so we stayed in Poland [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] to rely on God through the Rebbe. Number two, I was thinking lately, we had developed this idea-- you probably heard it-- [NON-ENGLISH], the 36 holy men. Because of them, the whole world is alive, the [NON-ENGLISH]. And the Rebbe was called the [NON-ENGLISH]. He was one of the pillars of this world. And you believed him. Otherwise, you were-- you know, it was a part of the article of faith. So it was a little bit more-- this, by itself, is a whole philosophy that we don't want to even to go into it. Because it's more questionable to give it in an interview and on-- it's a whole philosophy of life.

speaker: interviewer
text: I understand. In 1933, you said you moved?

speaker: interviewee
text: From Stojanow to Lemberg. Lemberg was a big city, about 120,000 Jews before the war. And when the war started in '39, it was about 170,000. When I mentioned the number, nobody questions it. But there is a few different numbers between 120,000 and 170,000 Jews-- again, divided into three parts. The same amount of Poles, the same amount of Ukrainians, the same amount of Jews. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And that was Lwow.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, what were the circumstances that made your parents move to Lwow? You had a business.

speaker: interviewee
text: We had a business. We bought an apartment house, a big apartment house, in [NON-ENGLISH]. And we moved to this apartment house.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did your father do there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Also in the wood business. He had-- he went to Lemberg practically every Sunday morning to Sunday night. Because at this day, you were sitting in this coffee house, where the [NON-ENGLISH], the center of dealing between different cities, and also for export business, you sat at your table. You drank coffee. And they came over and made business deals. So every Sunday, he left in the morning for Lwow and came back at home-- to our home in Sunday night. So it was-- going to Lwow, it was only 60 miles. But it was a trip by train. And he came-- and he came back Sunday night. So that was-- so it wasn't a strange town to go. So he settled in Lemberg in 1933.

speaker: interviewer
text: And tell me about your life and your education, for instance, in Lwow.

speaker: interviewee
text: My life was practically similar, but non-similar. I was, at this time, eight years old, going to school, going to cheder. So it was a full day work, six days a week. It wasn't the five-day week. So it was from 7:30 in the morning til about 5:00, 6:00 in the evening. You came home. And you had dinner. And you did your homework. And you went to sleep. The next day-- again. And so it wasn't so much different. I did not think about different. I did not. I remember the only thing is that you started to take from other children-- which was in Lwow, not in Stojanow-- about playing soccer, or so on, which my parents did not understand and did not want, because you got hot. And hot meant pneumonia, because you drank cold water, and you get pneumonia. And so it was against their will that you, as a kid, tried to play ball after school, but for half an hour, an hour, with the friends in school. But never the friends at home, and so on.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there more interaction in Lwow between Jews and non-Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not really. The Jews kept, as I said, automatically as a ghetto. Because you couldn't cut your hair. You did-- you had to have always a hat over, wearing a hat. You had to eat Kosher. You couldn't do anything with a non-Jew, or even with a Jew that was more assimilated.

speaker: interviewer
text: But the school that you went to-- did you go to a public school, as well as a cheder?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the beginning, my parents put me right in. Because you had to continue school. Because the law of Poland was you had to go to school. So I went right away to school. But in the neighborhood where we lived was not a pure Jewish neighborhood. And as such, I went to a school which was a mixed school, Gentile and Jews. As such, I had a little bit of a problem. Because the normal school was open on Saturday and closed Sunday. And in the Jewish neighborhood, even the government kept schools open on Sunday, but closed on Saturday, because it was a Jewish neighborhood. But I went to a school, because of my neighborhood, to a non-Jewish school. So the whole idea that, as my father used to say, he goes to the synagogue, and I'm going to school on Saturday. And it was forbidden for me to write on Saturday.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I went to school and did not write, or did not make any notes. And the other kids laughed at me, and so on. And I had my problem of even the name. You did not in Poland-- the Orthodox Jews did not change their name. So it was [? Leib ?] and not Leon, which was more assimilated a Polish name. And you couldn't have a Polish name. Tell me about-- you started to say that the children, the non-Jewish children, made fun of you. Do you remember any anti-Semitic incidents when you were a kid?

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, the idea of anti-Semitic or non-anti-Semitic-- I was just reading yesterday about-- about 1492, The Inquisition. It was ant-Semitic. The anti-Semitic-- mostly it was against Muslims, not Jewish. But we call it-- but anti-Semitic-- and a Muslim, we can say anti-Muslim is also anti-Semitic. We can call it anti-Semitic. But we, in our mind, anti-Semitic think all the time anti-Jewish. But it was not directed, and so on.

speaker: interviewer
text: For example, they-- they yelled at me, like for example, that I-- my name is Leon. And I said no, [? Lonik. ?] And they even threw some ink at me, and so on. Was this anti-Semitic? Because I wanted my name only [? Leib? ?] Or they said, no. Your name is [? Lonik-- ?] Leon, Lonik. And that's in Poland. You live in Poland, not in a Jewish state, and so on. Is this anti-Semitic? Yes. And no. You know, you can look at it different viewpoints, and so on. The whole idea is like certain-- so write about Germany and so on. In Germany, a Jew was a Jew at home. And outside, he was a German. And this is written by Joshua Heschel, and by different-- and in Poland, a Jew-- Orthodox Jew-- was at home and outside home. But in Germany, they behaved outside like everybody else. Where we did not want to. We said no, I'm Jewish. And this how I do it. That's all to it. Is it anti-Semitic or not? You know, it's what you want to [INAUDIBLE]. Tell me what your academic aspirations were and what your plans were as you got older. Were you going to continue your education past the normal elementary?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I was going to go to a high school. And my sister and my parents felt that we should get a high school education, where a normal, for them, public school was more than enough. And it was a progress of the society, and so on. But to speak about what do I really want aspiration, the war started when I was 14 years old. What is a 14, even today, aspiration, except a kid to be a garbage man or a policeman?

speaker: interviewer
text: Was the high school that you were going to-- was that a Jewish school?

speaker: interviewee
text: A Jewish high school, a Jewish gymnasium. There were two Jewish gymnasiums in Lwow. And the rest was general gymnasium.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. We'll stop here and continue in a moment.
